sections.heading: What is inversion?
sections.body: Inversion places the auxiliary verb (or 'be') before the subject, creating emphasis and a formal or dramatic tone. It's triggered by negative or restrictive adverbials at the beginning of a sentence.

sections.heading: Common inversion triggers
sections.body: Never, rarely, seldom, hardly, scarcely, barely, no sooner, not only, not until, on no account, in no way, under no circumstances, little (= not much), only + time expression.

sections.heading: Structure
sections.body: Trigger + auxiliary + subject + main verb. 'Never have I seen such courage.' 'Not only did she win, but she also broke the record.' 'Rarely does he make a mistake.' Compare normal order: 'He rarely makes a mistake.'

examples.english: Never have I seen such dedication.
Not only did she win, she broke a world record.
Rarely does he lose his temper.
Only after the meeting did I understand the plan.
Under no circumstances should you share this.

items: I have never heard such nonsense.
She not only apologized but also sent flowers.
He had barely arrived when the trouble started.
You should under no circumstances open that file.

quiz.question: Which sentence uses inversion correctly?
quiz.explanation: Inversion structure: negative trigger + auxiliary + subject + main verb: 'Never have I seen'.
